definition: A free JavaScript library made by Facebook (now Meta) for building user interfaces. Think of it like a set of LEGO bricks for websites - you snap together small pieces (components) to build something bigger.
example: React powers websites like Facebook, Instagram, Netflix, and Airbnb.

definition: A reusable piece of your website. Like how a car is made of parts (engine, wheels, seats), a website is made of components (header, button, card). Each component is its own little piece of code that handles one thing.
example: A 'Button' component can be reused everywhere on your site with different text and colors.

definition: A way of writing HTML inside JavaScript. It looks like HTML but it's actually JavaScript in disguise. React uses JSX to describe what the page should look like.
example: <Button color='blue'>Click Me</Button> is JSX - it looks like HTML but it's inside a JavaScript file.

definition: Short for 'properties'. These are inputs you pass to a component, like settings on a TV remote. Props tell a component what to display or how to behave. They flow one way: from parent to child.
example: <UserCard name='Alice' age={25} /> - 'name' and 'age' are props being passed to the UserCard component.

definition: Data that can change over time inside a component. Think of it like a component's memory. When state changes, React automatically updates what's shown on screen. For example, a counter button keeps track of how many times it's been clicked.
example: A shopping cart's item count is state - it changes when you add or remove items.

definition: Special functions that let you 'hook into' React features. They always start with 'use' (like useState, useEffect). Hooks let components remember things, run code at certain times, and share logic between components.
example: useState lets a component remember a value. useEffect lets it run code when something changes.

definition: A reusable function you write yourself that uses React hooks. It lets you extract and share logic between components. The name must start with 'use'. This keeps your components small (under 50 lines) and your logic testable.
example: useCountdown() could be a custom hook that handles countdown timer logic, used by any component that needs a timer.

definition: A lightweight copy of the real webpage that React keeps in memory. When something changes, React compares the virtual copy to the real page, figures out what's different, and only updates those specific parts. This makes updates very fast.
example: If you change one item in a list of 1000, React only updates that one item instead of rebuilding the entire list.

definition: Document Object Model. It's the browser's representation of your webpage as a tree of objects. Every HTML element (paragraph, button, image) is a node in this tree. JavaScript can read and change the DOM to update what you see.
example: When you right-click 'Inspect' on a webpage, you're looking at the DOM.

definition: HyperText Markup Language. The skeleton of every webpage. HTML tells the browser what content to show (headings, paragraphs, images, links) but not how it should look - that's CSS's job.
example:
Hello World

creates a big heading that says 'Hello World'.

definition: Cascading Style Sheets. The 'paint and decoration' of a webpage. CSS controls how things look: colors, sizes, spacing, fonts, animations. Without CSS, every website would look like a plain text document.
example: color: blue; font-size: 20px; makes text blue and 20 pixels tall.

definition: The programming language of the web. Every browser can run JavaScript. It makes websites interactive - handling button clicks, fetching data, showing/hiding things, validating forms. React is written in JavaScript.
example: When you click 'Add to Cart' and the cart count updates without reloading the page - that's JavaScript.

definition: JavaScript with added type checking. It's like JavaScript but with guardrails. You tell TypeScript what type of data to expect (string, number, etc.) and it catches mistakes before your code even runs. It prevents bugs.
example: function greet(name: string) - TypeScript ensures 'name' is always text, never accidentally a number.

definition: TypeScript + JSX combined. It's files ending in .tsx that contain both TypeScript code and JSX markup. This is the standard file format for React components when using TypeScript.
example: A file like Button.tsx contains a React component written with TypeScript type safety.

definition: JavaScript Object Notation. A simple format for storing and sending data. It uses curly braces {} for objects and square brackets [] for lists. It's human-readable and is the standard way websites send data back and forth.
example: { "name": "Alice", "age": 25 } is JSON representing a person.

definition: Application Programming Interface. A way for two pieces of software to talk to each other. Think of it like a waiter in a restaurant: your frontend (the customer) tells the API (the waiter) what it wants, and the API brings back data from the backend (the kitchen).
example: When a weather app shows today's forecast, it got that data by asking a weather API.

definition: REpresentational State Transfer. A set of rules for how APIs should work. REST APIs use standard HTTP methods: GET (read data), POST (create data), PUT (update data), DELETE (remove data). Most web APIs follow REST.
example: GET /api/users fetches all users. POST /api/users creates a new user.

definition: The server-side part of a website that users never see. It handles data storage, business logic, authentication, and serving data to the frontend. Think of it as the kitchen in a restaurant - the work happens behind the scenes.
example: When you log in, the backend checks your password, creates a session, and tells the frontend 'you're in'.

definition: The part of a website that users see and interact with in their browser. Everything visual: buttons, text, images, forms, animations. React is a frontend library.
example: The login form you type into is the frontend. Checking if the password is correct is the backend.

definition: A lightweight Python web framework for building backend APIs. It's simple and beginner-friendly - you can build a working API in just a few lines of code. Flask handles receiving requests and sending responses.
example: @app.route('/api/hello') creates an endpoint that responds when someone visits /api/hello.

definition: A framework built on top of React that adds features like routing (pages), server-side rendering, and file-based organization. Think of React as the engine and Next.js as the car - it provides structure and extra features around React.
example: Creating a file at app/about/page.tsx automatically creates a /about page on your website.

definition: A library of pre-built, professionally designed React components. Instead of building buttons, cards, and forms from scratch, you import ready-made ones. It follows Google's Material Design guidelines.
example: import Button from '@mui/material/Button' gives you a styled, accessible button component instantly.

definition: A library for managing application state (data) in a central place. Instead of each component managing its own data, Redux keeps everything in one 'store' that any component can read from or write to.
example: A shopping cart state in Redux can be accessed from the header, the cart page, and the checkout - all reading the same data.

definition: The official, recommended way to use Redux. It simplifies Redux by removing the boilerplate code. It includes utilities for creating stores, slices, and async logic. Always use Redux Toolkit, never plain Redux.
example: createSlice() from Redux Toolkit lets you define state + actions in one place, instead of writing separate files.

definition: In Redux, the store is the single central object that holds all your application's state. Think of it like a database for your frontend. Every component can read from the store and dispatch actions to change it.
example: The store might contain { theme: 'dark', user: { name: 'Alice' }, cart: [item1, item2] }.

definition: A portion of the Redux store that handles one feature. Each slice has its own state, reducers (update functions), and actions. Slices keep your code organized by feature rather than by function.
example: themeSlice handles dark/light mode. examSlice handles quiz state. Each is independent.

definition: Node Package Manager. A tool for installing and managing JavaScript libraries (packages). It comes with Node.js. When you run 'npm install', it downloads all the libraries your project needs.
example: npm install @mui/material downloads the Material UI library into your project.

definition: A way to run JavaScript outside the browser (on your computer or a server). It's what makes tools like npm, Next.js dev server, and build tools work. You need Node.js installed to develop React websites.
example: When you run 'npm run dev', Node.js is what actually starts the development server.

definition: A file in your project root that lists all the libraries (dependencies) your project uses, plus scripts for common tasks. It's like a recipe card - it tells anyone exactly what ingredients (packages) and instructions (scripts) are needed.
example: "dependencies": { "react": "^19.0.0" } means your project needs React version 19 or later.

definition: A tool that checks your code for common mistakes, bad practices, and style issues as you write. Think of it like a spelling and grammar checker for code. It can automatically fix many issues for you.
example: ESLint will warn you if you create a variable but never use it, or if you forget to handle an error.

definition: A version control system that tracks changes to your code over time. It's like 'Save As' with superpowers - you can go back to any previous version, work on features without breaking the main code, and collaborate with others.
example: If you break something, 'git checkout' can take your code back to when it was working.

definition: A website that hosts Git repositories (code projects) online. It's like Google Drive for code. Developers use it to store code, collaborate with others, track issues, and share open-source projects.
example: React itself is hosted on GitHub - anyone can read its source code and suggest improvements.

definition: A tool that packages your application and everything it needs (libraries, settings, runtime) into a container. Containers run the same everywhere, solving the 'works on my machine' problem. Like a shipping container that works on any ship.
example: docker-compose up starts your entire app (frontend + backend + database) with one command.

definition: A tool for running multiple Docker containers together. You define all your services (frontend, backend, database) in one YAML file, and start everything with a single command.
example: docker-compose.yml might define a frontend on port 3000, backend on port 5000, and a database on port 5432.

definition: Short for 'internationalization' (i + 18 letters + n). The process of designing your app so it CAN support multiple languages. This means using translation keys instead of hardcoded text, and formatting dates/numbers for different locales.
example: Instead of writing 'Hello', you write t('greeting') which shows 'Hello' in English, 'Bonjour' in French, etc.

definition: Short for 'localization' (l + 10 letters + n). The actual work of translating your app into a specific language and adapting it to local conventions (date formats, currency symbols, reading direction).
example: Creating a French translation file with all your app's text translated to French is localization.

definition: A code identifying a language and optionally a region. It tells the app which language and formatting rules to use.
example: 'en' = English, 'en-US' = American English, 'fr' = French, 'ja' = Japanese.

definition: Next.js 13+'s routing system. It uses the file system: creating a folder with a page.tsx file creates a route. Supports layouts, loading states, error boundaries, and server components out of the box.
example: app/about/page.tsx becomes the /about page. app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx becomes /blog/any-post-name.

definition: A URL path that maps to a page in your app. Each route shows different content. Routes can have dynamic segments (like a blog post ID) and nested layouts.
example: /about is a route showing the about page. /products/123 is a dynamic route showing product #123.

definition: Making your website look good on all screen sizes - phones, tablets, laptops, and big monitors. Instead of building separate mobile and desktop sites, responsive design adapts the same site to any screen.
example: A 3-column layout on desktop might stack into a single column on mobile - same content, different arrangement.

definition: The process of putting your website on the internet so anyone can visit it. You take your code from your computer and upload it to a server. Services like Vercel and Netlify make this very easy.
example: Running 'git push' can automatically deploy your site to Vercel in about 30 seconds.

definition: A cloud platform that hosts websites, especially Next.js apps. It was created by the same team that makes Next.js. It offers free hosting for personal projects and automatically deploys when you push code to GitHub.
example: Connect your GitHub repo to Vercel, and every push to main automatically updates your live website.

definition: A specific URL on a server that accepts requests and returns data. Each endpoint serves a specific purpose. Think of endpoints as different counters at a government office - each handles a specific type of request.
example: GET /api/users is an endpoint that returns a list of users. POST /api/login is an endpoint for logging in.

definition: Code that runs between receiving a request and sending a response. It can modify requests, check authentication, redirect users, or add headers. Think of it as a security guard who checks your ID before letting you into a building.
example: Auth middleware checks if a user is logged in before allowing access to protected pages.

definition: The process of verifying who someone is (are you really Alice?). Usually done with a username/email and password, but can also use social logins (Google, GitHub), fingerprints, or security keys.
example: Typing your email and password to log into a website is authentication.

definition: JSON Web Token. A compact, secure way to transmit information between two parties. After you log in, the server gives you a JWT (like a wristband at a concert). You show it with each request to prove you're authenticated.
example: After login, the server returns a JWT. Your frontend includes it in every API request: Authorization: Bearer eyJhbG...

definition: A value stored outside your code that configures how your app behaves. Used for secrets (API keys, database passwords) and settings that change between development and production. Never put secrets directly in code.
example: DATABASE_URL=postgres://... in a .env file tells your app where to find the database.

definition: A lightweight programming language popular in game development. If you've played Roblox, you've likely used Lua (or its derivative Luau). It can be embedded into web apps to let users write simple scripts.
example: Roblox games are scripted in Lua. The language is small, fast, and easy to learn.

definition: A binary format that lets you run code written in languages like C, Rust, or Lua directly in the browser at near-native speed. It's how you can embed a Lua engine into a website.
example: A Lua interpreter compiled to WebAssembly can run Lua scripts inside a web browser.

definition: A text-based interface for giving commands to your computer. Also called command line, console, or shell. You type commands instead of clicking buttons. Essential for web development (running servers, installing packages, using git).
example: Typing 'npm run dev' in the terminal starts your development server.

definition: Integrated Development Environment. A fancy text editor designed for writing code, with features like syntax highlighting, auto-completion, error detection, and debugging tools. VS Code is the most popular free IDE.
example: VS Code highlights your code in different colors, suggests completions, and catches errors as you type.

definition: A set of design tokens (colors, fonts, spacing, shadows) that define the visual style of your entire app. By changing the theme, you can instantly change the look of every component. Supports dark mode and light mode.
example: Changing primary.main from blue to green in your theme file changes every primary-colored element on the site.

definition: A library that automatically saves Redux state to localStorage (browser storage) and restores it when the user comes back. Without it, all state would be lost on page refresh.
example: Your dark mode preference and exam history survive page refreshes because Redux Persist saves them to localStorage.

definition: The process of converting your React components into actual HTML that the browser displays. Can happen on the server (SSR) or in the browser (client-side). React re-renders components when their state or props change.
example: When you click a 'like' button and the count increases, React re-renders that component to show the new number.

definition: In TypeScript, a way to define the shape of an object - what properties it has and what types they are. Like a blueprint that says 'a User must have a name (string) and age (number)'. Helps catch errors at compile time.
example: interface User { name: string; age: number; } ensures every User object has the right shape.

definition: A library or package your project relies on. Listed in package.json. Dependencies are other people's code that you use to avoid reinventing the wheel (React, Material UI, Redux are all dependencies).
example: React is a dependency of your project. Material UI is a dependency. They're installed with 'npm install'.

definition: The process of converting your development code into optimized code ready for production. Building compresses files, removes unused code, and bundles everything into efficient packages. Like compiling a manuscript into a printed book.
example: 'npm run build' creates an optimized version of your site in a .next folder, ready to deploy.

definition: A development feature where changes you make to code instantly appear in the browser without losing the current page state. You save a file, and the change shows up in milliseconds. Massively speeds up development.
example: Change a button's color in your code, save, and see it update in the browser instantly - no manual refresh needed.

definition: Making websites usable by everyone, including people with disabilities. This includes screen reader support, keyboard navigation, sufficient color contrast, and proper HTML semantics. 'a11y' is short for 'accessibility' (a + 11 letters + y).
example: Adding alt text to images means screen readers can describe the image to visually impaired users.

definition: Search Engine Optimization. Techniques to help your website appear higher in Google search results. Includes proper HTML structure, meta tags, fast loading times, mobile-friendliness, and good content.
example: Adding a descriptive <title> and <meta description> helps Google understand what your page is about.
